full_response:
Let K be King Arthur’s rest frame.  
The west and east ends of the track are at x = 0 and x = L, and the “finish line’’ is the midpoint x = L/2.  
At t = 0 (in K) the King’s bugle sounds and the two knights are instantaneously boosted to speed v, Knight #1 toward +x, Knight #2 toward –x.  
In K both riders therefore meet at the midpoint at

 t_f = L/(2v), x_f = L/2,            (1)

so the King judges the race to be fair.

Now examine the same events in the inertial frame K′ that is instantaneously comoving with Knight #2 at the moment he starts.  
Choose the origin of both frames to be Knight #2’s start event S (t = 0, x = L in K and t′ = 0, x′ = 0 in K′).  
With the usual Lorentz transformation (K′ moves with +v relative to K)

 t′ = γ[(t − 0) − (v/c²)(x − L)], x′ = γ[(x − L) − v(t − 0)],  (2)

where γ = 1/√(1 − β²) and β = v/c.

Start of Knight #1  
(event S₁: t = 0, x = 0):

 t′₁ = γ[0 − (v/c²)(0 − L)] = γβL/c > 0.          (3)

Thus Knight #2 sees that he himself starts first; Knight #1 does not begin until a later time t′₁.

Finish event F (both riders and the finish line coincide):

 t_f = L/(2v), x_f = L/2 in K.  
Insert into (2):

 t′_F = γ[ L/(2v) − (v/c²)(L/2 − L) ]
    = γ[ L/(2v) + βL/(2c) ] > 0.          (4)

Because F is a single space-time point, both riders reach it simultaneously in every frame, K′ included.

Conclusion in Knight #2’s frame  
• Knight #2 starts at t′ = 0.  
• Knight #1 starts later, at t′ = γβL/c.  
• However, the finish line itself is moving toward Knight #1 and away from Knight #2 with speed v, so Knight #1 has a correspondingly shorter distance to cover.  
The two effects cancel exactly: both riders still arrive together at event F.  

Therefore Knight #2 agrees that, despite the relativity-of-simultaneity head-start he apparently enjoys, the race is perfectly fair.

\boxed{\text{Yes.  In Knight \#2’s frame he starts earlier, but the finish line is moving toward Knight \#1, so both riders still reach it at the same instant; he therefore judges the race to be fair.}}

Confidence: 65%
extracted_answer: \text{Yes.  In Knight \#2’s frame he starts earlier, but the finish line is moving toward Knight \#1, so both riders still reach it at the same instant; he therefore judges the race to be fair.}
